message:
feat(cli,core,layout,parser,render,wasm): major expansion of layout algorithms, rendering, and CI infrastructure

CLI: Add minimize subcommand for diagram minimization. Extend main.rs
with 824 new lines of CLI functionality. New debug and spectral bug
reproduction tests.

Core: Add art (adaptive radix tree), CGA (conformal geometric algebra),
constraints solver, leapfrog join, lens tests, quotient filter, and
succinct data structure modules.

Layout: Add cache-oblivious layout, delta debugging, e-graph ordering,
persistence layer, polyhedral optimization, spatial indexing, spectral
layout, and cycle removal test modules.

Parser: Improve DOT parser edge cases, IR builder robustness, and
Mermaid parser handling of complex diagram types.

Rendering: Expand canvas renderer with new shape support (88 lines).
Extend SVG renderer with attribute and style improvements (117 lines).
Fix terminal renderer edge cases.

WASM: Add 87 lines of new WASM binding exports.

CI: Add perf baseline, release gate overrides, release signoff config,
and SLO definitions.

- 1512 insertions across 14 tracked files
- 18 new source modules across core, layout, CLI
- Evidence run artifacts for web compliance

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-20fq.5","title":"Benchmark: incremental vs full recompute on 1000-node diagram edits","description":"Quantify the performance improvement of incremental subgraph re-layout vs full recompute.

## Benchmark Design
1. Generate reference diagrams: 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000 nodes with varying density (sparse tree, medium DAG, dense cyclic).
2. Define edit workloads: (a) single-node add, (b) single-edge add, (c) 5-node cluster edit, (d) 10% bulk edit.
3. For each (diagram, edit) pair, measure: (a) full recompute wall time, (b) incremental update wall time, (c) dependency graph overhead, (d) dirty subgraph count, (e) memory delta.
4. Compute speedup ratio and plot as function of diagram size and edit size.
5. Identify crossover point: at what diagram size does incremental become faster than full recompute?
6. Profile hotspots in the incremental path — is it the dirty-marking, the subgraph extraction, or the stitching?

## Success Criteria
- Incremental is >= 5x faster than full recompute for single-node edits on 1000+ node diagrams.
- Incremental is >= 2x faster for 5-node cluster edits on 1000+ node diagrams.
- No regression for diagrams < 50 nodes (bypass path).

## Output
- Criterion benchmark suite in fm-layout/benches/.
- Markdown report with charts (can be generated by benchmark harness).
- CI budget: incremental single-node edit on 1000-node graph < 10ms.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:21.762613249Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:36.505410188Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmark","incremental","layout","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.5","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:21.762613249Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.5","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:36.505338504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-29h","title":"Implement specialized state diagram layout with nested states","description":"State diagrams need: (1) Start/end pseudo-states: filled circle for start, bullseye for end. (2) State nodes: rounded rects with state name. (3) Nested/composite states: state containing child states (uses cluster layout). (4) Transitions: directed edges with event labels, guard conditions, and actions. (5) Fork/join bars: horizontal bars for concurrent state entry/exit. (6) Choice pseudo-state: diamond shape for conditional branching. (7) History states: circled H for shallow history, circled H* for deep history. Layout should emphasize the flow from start to end, with nested states properly contained. Depends on: fm-core, fm-parser, fm-layout.

- Diagnostics are actionable: failures/warnings include precise context and suggested remediation.
- Determinism and stability expectations are verified for affected outputs (layout/render/serialization as applicable).

IrBuilder gains begin_state_cluster/end_state_cluster. 4 parser tests added.  {"id":"bd-2b4.1","title":"Build fm-cli with render command and multi-format output","description":"Build fm-cli/src/main.rs with clap-based CLI. Commands: (1) render: read mermaid from stdin or file, output SVG/PNG/terminal. Flags: --format svg|png|term|ascii, --theme name, --output file, --width/--height. (2) parse: output IR as JSON for tooling/debugging. (3) detect: show detected diagram type and confidence. (4) validate: check input for errors, report diagnostics. (5) watch: re-render on file change (notify crate). (6) serve: start local HTTP server with live-reload playground (tiny embedded server). PNG output via resvg (SVG-to-PNG rasterization in pure Rust). Terminal output uses fm-render-term. Dependencies: clap, fm-core, fm-parser, fm-layout, fm-render-svg, fm-render-term, resvg (optional, for PNG), notify (optional, for watch mode).

 {"id":"bd-2b4.3","title":"Define structured diagnostics contract and implement fm-cli validate JSON mode","description":"Implement a formal diagnostics contract and CLI surface for machine-readable validation results.

Scope:
1. Add a stable diagnostics payload schema in fm-core (error code, severity, span, source line/column, rule_id, confidence, remediation hint).
2. Implement fm-cli validate --format json and --format pretty with deterministic field ordering.
3. Ensure parser/layout/render warnings are merged into one sorted diagnostics stream.
4. Add --fail-on severity threshold for CI gating.
5. Emit optional diagnostics artifact file via --diagnostics-out for automation.

Why this improves the system:
- Makes FrankenMermaid easy to embed into CI, editors, and automation pipelines.
- Converts freeform warnings into actionable, typed remediation data.

 {"id":"bd-2b4.4","title":"Add end-to-end trace bundle export and replay tooling","description":"Add a trace bundle export mode that captures full pipeline evidence for debugging and performance triage.

Scope:
1. Implement --trace-json and --trace-dir in fm-cli.
2. Capture normalized artifacts: input metadata, detection output, parser diagnostics, layout trace, render stats.
3. Include deterministic run IDs and schema version for machine tooling.
4. Add redaction options for sensitive input content.
5. Provide replay helper command to re-run from a trace bundle.

Why this improves the system:
- Speeds up root-cause analysis and enables reproducible bug reports.

 {"id":"bd-2b4.5","title":"Add source-map pipeline from Mermaid input spans to rendered output","description":"Expose source maps linking rendered elements back to source spans for debuggability and tooling.

Scope:
1. Attach source span metadata to IR nodes/edges and carry through layout/render pipeline.
2. Emit optional source map artifact mapping output element IDs to input spans.
3. Add CLI flags to include/exclude embedded source-map metadata in SVG.
4. Validate source map integrity for malformed and recovered inputs.
5. Provide integration hook for editor highlighting workflows.

Why this improves the system:
- Makes diagnostics precise and unlocks richer IDE/editor integrations.

 {"id":"bd-32su","title":"Spectral Graph Partitioning for Hierarchical Layout Decomposition","description":"## Spectral Graph Partitioning for Hierarchical Layout Decomposition

Use spectral graph partitioning (eigendecomposition of the graph Laplacian) to decompose large diagrams into well-separated subgraphs for hierarchical layout, enabling divide-and-conquer layout strategies that scale to thousands of nodes.

## Mathematical Foundation
The graph Laplacian L = D - A (degree matrix minus adjacency matrix) encodes the graph's connectivity structure. Its eigenvalues and eigenvectors reveal graph structure:
- λ_1 = 0 always (constant eigenvector)
- λ_2 (Fiedler value) = algebraic connectivity; its eigenvector (Fiedler vector) provides the optimal bisection
- λ_k reveals k-way partition quality

**Spectral bisection algorithm:**
1. Compute Fiedler vector v_2 (eigenvector of second-smallest eigenvalue of L)
2. Partition nodes: S_1 = {i : v_2[i] < median(v_2)}, S_2 = {i : v_2[i] >= median(v_2)}
3. This minimizes the normalized cut: NCut(S_1, S_2) = cut(S_1, S_2) * (1/vol(S_1) + 1/vol(S_2))
4. Recursively bisect for k-way partitioning

**For diagram layout:**
- Partition large diagram into 4-8 clusters
- Layout each cluster independently (parallel execution possible)
- Stitch clusters together using inter-cluster edge routing
- Result: O(n/k) layout per partition instead of O(n), with inter-cluster overhead

**Eigensolver choice:**
- For n < 500: dense eigendecomposition (O(n³))
- For n >= 500: Lanczos iteration (O(n*k*iter), typically iter < 100)
- Rust crate: nalgebra (dense) or sprs + arpack-like (sparse Lanczos)

## Key Papers
- Fiedler, "Algebraic Connectivity of Graphs" (Czech Math Journal 1973)
- Shi & Malik, "Normalized Cuts and Image Segmentation" (IEEE TPAMI 2000) — NCut
- Von Luxburg, "A Tutorial on Spectral Clustering" (Statistics and Computing 2007)
- Koren, "Drawing Graphs by Eigenvectors" (Computers & Mathematics 2005) — spectral layout

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Add `nalgebra` crate for eigendecomposition (already likely a transitive dependency).
2. Implement `SpectralPartitioner` that takes a graph and returns k partitions.
3. Compute Fiedler vector via Lanczos iteration for sparse graphs.
4. Implement recursive bisection for k-way partitioning.
5. Integrate with layout pipeline: for graphs > threshold (e.g., 200 nodes), spectral partition first, then layout each partition.
6. Implement inter-cluster edge routing (edges crossing partition boundaries).
7. Benchmark: layout time for 1000, 5000, 10000 node graphs with/without partitioning.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spectral bisection produces balanced partitions (each partition within 40-60% of total)
- [ ] Fiedler vector computed correctly (verified against known graph spectra)
- [ ] Layout quality of partitioned approach within 10% of monolithic layout
- [ ] Layout time improvement >= 3x for 5000+ node graphs
- [ ] Inter-cluster edges routed without visual artifacts
- [ ] Threshold tuning: spectral partitioning skipped for small graphs (< 200 nodes)
- [ ] Decision contract: adopt if speedup >= 2x AND quality within 15%","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:46:53.071379760Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T20:48:00.183552929Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T20:48:00.183265365Z","close_reason":"Implemented spectral graph partitioning module (crates/fm-layout/src/spectral.rs) with: Laplacian construction from IR edges, Fiedler vector computation via nalgebra eigendecomposition, spectral bisection with balance constraints, recursive k-way partitioning, partition mapping for layout pipeline integration, threshold-based activation, normalized cut quality metric. 16 unit tests covering Laplacian properties, Fiedler vector correctness, barbell/clique/star/complete/path graph partitioning, balance constraints, partition mapping roundtrip, and quality metrics. All quality gates pass (check, clippy, fmt).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","graph-partitioning","layout","spectral"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32su","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:09.332893165Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32su","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:52.027515765Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

+
+# Emergency release-gate overrides are exceptional and must stay time-boxed.
+# Every entry requires:

+# - an explicit reason
+# - exact gate scope
+# - expiration

@@ -0,0 +1,59 @@
+schema_version = 1
+
+[[checklist]]
+id = "blocking-quality-gates"
+title = "Blocking quality gates pass"
+owner = "runtime"
+source = "gate_summary"
+criterion = "No uncovered failing gates remain after valid overrides are applied."
+playbook = "Inspect uncovered_failing_gates, fix the failing guard, or add a valid time-boxed override in .ci/release-gate-overrides.toml."
+
+[[checklist]]
+id = "override-ledger"
+title = "Override ledger remains explicit and valid"
+owner = "release-manager"
+source = "override_summary"
+criterion = "The override summary parses cleanly and all active overrides remain authorized, time-boxed, and scoped."
+playbook = "Run `cargo run -p fm-cli --bin evidence -- --root . verify-overrides`, then expire or repair any bad override entries before release."
+
+[[checklist]]
+id = "demo-evidence"
+title = "Hosted demo evidence remains stable"
+owner = "demo"
+source = "demo_evidence"
+criterion = "Static and React release summaries both validate with replay bundles and full normalized-log stability."
+playbook = "Rerun the release-grade browser suites and inspect the replay bundles plus determinism summaries for the first divergent scenario/profile pair."
+
+[[validation_matrix]]
+id = "static-web"
+title = "Static /web release replay"
+owner = "demo"
+source = "demo_static"
+surface = "web"
+host_kind = "static-web"
+criterion = "Every static-web scenario/profile group keeps stable normalized logs and retains a replay manifest."
+playbook = "Rerun `scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py` for `/web`, then inspect `artifacts/evidence/demo/static` and `artifacts/evidence/demo/static-replay`."
+
+[[validation_matrix]]
+id = "react-web"
+title = "React /web_react release replay"
+owner = "demo"
+source = "demo_react"
+surface = "web_react"
+host_kind = "react-web"
+criterion = "Every react-web scenario/profile group keeps stable normalized logs and retains a replay manifest."
+playbook = "Rerun `scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py` for `/web_react`, then inspect `artifacts/evidence/demo/react` and `artifacts/evidence/demo/react-replay`."
+
+[[risks]]
+id = "override-drift"
+title = "Emergency override drift"
+owner = "release-manager"
+trigger = "Overrides remain active without fix/retro follow-through."
+mitigation_playbook = "Expire the override, prioritize the linked fix bead, and close the retro bead before the next release window."
+
+[[risks]]
+id = "browser-replay-drift"
+title = "Hosted replay drift"
+owner = "demo"
+trigger = "Normalized logs stop matching across repeated static or React runs."
+mitigation_playbook = "Use the replay bundle to isolate the scenario/profile pair, then inspect the captured HTML and structured logs for the first divergent run."

@@ -0,0 +1,52 @@
+# React `/web_react` release-grade replay suite
+
+This bundle extends the earlier React smoke run into a retained replay package with exact rerun commands.
+
+## What was run
+
+```bash
+python3 scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py \
+  --bead-id bd-2u0.5.11.4 \
+  --repo-root . \
+  --output-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.11.4/react \
+  --route-prefix /web_react \
+  --surface web_react \
+  --host-kind react-web \
+  --scenario-prefix react-web \
+  --chromium /snap/bin/chromium \
+  --repeat 5 \
+  --replay-bundle-dir evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.11.4/replay
+```
+
+Focused replay validation also passed:
+
+```bash
+python3 scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py \
+  --bead-id bd-2u0.5.11.4 \
+  --repo-root . \
+  --output-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.11.4/replay-check \
+  --route-prefix /web_react \
+  --surface web_react \
+  --host-kind react-web \
+  --scenario-prefix react-web \
+  --chromium /snap/bin/chromium \
+  --repeat 1 \
+  --scenario-id static-web-determinism-check \
+  --profile-id desktop-default
+```
+
+## Artifacts
+
+- replay summary: `react/2026-04-01T17-54-52Z__determinism__summary.json`
+- replay manifest: `replay/replay_manifest.json`
+- replay helper script: `replay/replay_suite.sh`
+- per-run HTML dumps and logs under `react/react-web-*/<profile>/`
+
+## Verdict
+
+- 4 scenarios x 3 profiles x 5 repeats completed
+- `stable_normalized_log: true` for every scenario/profile group
+- determinism-check produced a stable output hash across all five repeats for every profile
+- compare/export, diagnostics recovery, and presenter tour remain semantically stable but byte-level DOM hashes still drift across repeats
+
+That split is now explicit in the retained evidence and replay bundle, which is the main closure for this bead.

@@ -0,0 +1,22 @@
+# React `/web_react` Replay
+
+Use the shared showcase harness to validate the current `/web_react` host adapter:
+
+```bash
+python3 scripts/showcase_harness.py validate-react-web \
+  --entry web_react/index.html \
+  --headers web_react/_headers \
+  --contract evidence/contracts/showcase_react_embedding_contract.md
+```
+
+What it validates:
+
+- the route bootstraps the standalone showcase artifact rather than forking host semantics
+- the injected host markers switch to `react-web`
+- the route defines a stable `showcase-react-root`
+- deep-link/cache rules exist for `/web_react` and `/web_react/*`
+- the checked-in React embedding contract still matches the route surface
+
+Current evidence artifact:
+
+- `react-web-entrypoint`

@@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+# React `/web_react` E2E Replay
+
+Use the shared headless Chromium runner to exercise the hosted React route with the same scenario corpus used by `/web`:
+
+```bash
+python3 scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py \
+  --bead-id bd-2u0.5.8.3.3 \
+  --repo-root . \
+  --output-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.3.3 \
+  --route-prefix /web_react \
+  --surface web_react \
+  --host-kind react-web \
+  --scenario-prefix react-web \
+  --chromium /snap/bin/chromium
+```
+
+What it does:
+
+- serves the repository root over a local HTTP server
+- opens `/web_react` in headless Chromium with query-restored scenarios
+- captures post-JavaScript DOM dumps as reviewable HTML artifacts
+- emits schema-valid JSON logs with `surface=web_react` and `host_kind=react-web`
+- reuses the same determinism summary flow as `/web`
+
+Current scenarios:
+
+- `react-web-compare-export`
+- `react-web-diagnostics-recovery`
+- `react-web-determinism-check`
+- `react-web-presenter-tour`

@@ -0,0 +1,55 @@
+# bd-2u0.5.8.4 parity evidence
+
+This directory captures the cross-surface parity harness for `/web` and `/web_react`.
+
+## What was run
+
+```bash
+python3 scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py \
+  --bead-id bd-2u0.5.8.4 \
+  --repo-root . \
+  --output-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.4/static \
+  --route-prefix /web \
+  --surface web \
+  --host-kind static-web \
+  --scenario-prefix static-web \
+  --chromium /snap/bin/chromium \
+  --repeat 2
+
+python3 scripts/run_static_web_e2e.py \
+  --bead-id bd-2u0.5.8.4 \
+  --repo-root . \
+  --output-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.4/react \
+  --route-prefix /web_react \
+  --surface web_react \
+  --host-kind react-web \
+  --scenario-prefix react-web \
+  --chromium /snap/bin/chromium \
+  --repeat 2
+
+python3 scripts/showcase_harness.py compare-host-parity \
+  --static-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.4/static \
+  --react-root evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.4/react \
+  --report-out evidence/runs/web/bd-2u0.5.8.4/2026-04-01T17-49-30Z__parity__report.json
+```
+
+## Verdict
+
+- `ok: true`
+- compared 12 scenario/profile pairs
+- no missing scenario coverage on either host
+- no strict parity mismatches
+
+## Acceptable deltas
+
+The parity harness treats the following as expected host-level differences:
+
+- `surface`
+- `host_kind`
+- `output_artifact_hash`
+- `pass_fail_reason`
+- `parse_ms`, `layout_ms`, `render_ms` within the configured tolerance
+- `input_hash`, because route prefixes differ
+- `config_hash`, `revision`, and `trace_id`
+
+Strict parity is still required for renderer/theme selection, diagnostic counts, degradation tier, runtime mode, fallback state, and determinism status.
